scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE Civil Judge (Senior Division)
AT Bengaluru

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT WITH COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute this suit. The Defendant is a person sui juris and can be sued. The suit property is situated within Bengaluru, and the Defendant resides within HSR Layout, Bengaluru, which is within the territorial jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court. The value of the suit is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court. This Court has jurisdiction to try this suit under Section 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, as the suit is for determination of a right to immovable property situated within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Court. The suit is filed in the Court of the lowest grade competent to try it.

LIMITATION

2. The suit is within limitation under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date fixed for performance of the contract. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

FACTS OF THE AGREEMENT

3. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff entered into an Agreement to Sell with the Defendant for the purchase of a residential flat bearing No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft. The total consideration agreed upon for the said property was Rs. 85,00,000/-. The terms of the agreement included the payment of the balance consideration on or before 10.07.2024 against the execution of the sale deed.

4. On the date of the agreement, i.e., 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant via RTGS. The transaction is evidenced by the bank receipt bearing UTR No. HDFC20240110987654. The original Agreement to Sell and the RTGS receipt are marked as Annexure A and Annexure B respectively.

5. The Plaintiff was required to pay the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- on or before 10.07.2024. The Plaintiff has maintained sufficient funds to discharge this obligation and has kept the amount ready for payment since the date of the agreement. The Plaintiff has always intended to complete the transaction according to the terms stipulated in the agreement.

PLAINTIFF'S READINESS AND WILLINGNESS

6. The Plaintiff has always been and continues to be ready and willing to perform her part of the contract. The Plaintiff has arranged the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- and keeps the funds available in her bank accounts to tender the same upon demand. The Plaintiff has repeatedly communicated her willingness to proceed with the execution of the sale deed to the Defendant.

7. The Plaintiff has taken all necessary steps to prepare the documents required for the registration of the sale deed, including arranging for the payment of stamp duty and registration fees. The Plaintiff offered to pay the balance consideration and execute the sale deed within the timeline agreed upon. The Plaintiff has not committed any breach of the terms of the agreement and is capable of fulfilling all her obligations under the contract.

DEFENDANT'S REFUSAL TO PERFORM

8. On 05.06.2024, the Defendant sent a letter to the Plaintiff expressly refusing to execute the sale deed. The Defendant cited the receipt of a higher offer from a third party as the reason for his refusal to honor the agreement entered into with the Plaintiff. This letter is marked as Annexure C.

9. Aggrieved by the Defendant's refusal, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to execute the sale deed and complete the transaction as per the agreement. The legal notice along with the postal receipt is marked as Annexure D. The Defendant has failed to comply with the legal notice and has maintained his refusal to perform the contract.

10. The Defendant's actions constitute a clear breach of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024. Despite the Plaintiff's readiness and willingness to perform, the Defendant has evaded his obligation to transfer the suit property to the Plaintiff.

LEGAL BASIS

11. The Plaintiff is entitled to specific performance under Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, which mandates that contracts relating to immovable property shall be specifically enforced unless exceptions under Section 14 or Section 16 apply. The contract in question is for the transfer of immovable property and does not fall under the exceptions where specific performance is barred.

12. Under Section 16 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, the Plaintiff has pleaded and proved continuous readiness and willingness to perform the contract from the date thereof until the hearing of the suit. The Plaintiff has arranged the funds and offered performance, satisfying the requirements of Section 16(c). The contract is valid and enforceable under Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

13. The agreement is admissible for the purpose of proving part performance under Section 53A of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882. Time is not of the essence in contracts for immovable property unless expressly stipulated, and the Plaintiff is within her rights to seek performance despite the Defendant's unilateral refusal. The Plaintiff is also entitled to compensation under Section 21 and possession under Section 22 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 if specific performance is granted.

14. This Court has jurisdiction under Section 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. There is no unusual hardship to the Defendant that would warrant refusal of relief. The Plaintiff is therefore entitled to the reliefs sought under the cited provisions.

CAUSE OF ACTION

15. The cause of action first arose on 10.07.2024, the date fixed for performance of the contract under Article 54 of the Limitation Act, 1963, when the Defendant failed to execute the sale deed. The cause of action also arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant sent the letter refusing to execute the sale deed, and continues to subsist as the Defendant has failed to perform his obligations despite the legal notice dated 20.06.2024.

16. The suit is governed by Article 54 of the Limitation Act, 1963, which prescribes a period of three years from the date fixed for performance or when the Plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

17. The right to sue arose within the jurisdiction of this Court and within the limitation period prescribed by law.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

18. For the purpose of court fee and jurisdiction, the suit is valued at Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty Five Lakhs Only), being the total consideration agreed upon in the Agreement to Sell.

19. The requisite court fee of {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid on the plaint under the applicable Court Fees Act. The plaint is properly stamped and tendered with the requisite court fee.

20. The valuation is made for the purpose of invoking the jurisdiction of this Court and payment of court fees as required by law.

PRAYER

21. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

(a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024, directing the Defendant to execute the sale deed in favor of the Plaintiff;
(b) Direct the Defendant to deliver vacant and peaceful possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff upon execution of the sale deed;
(c) In the alternative, award compensation under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 in lieu of or i

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

C.S. No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A company registered under the Companies Act, 1956/2013,
Having its registered office at: MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon, aged 40 years.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A limited liability partnership registered under the LLP Act, 2008,
Having its registered office at: Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh, aged 48 years.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT UNDER SECTION 73 OF THE INDIAN CONTRACT ACT, 1872

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The suit is valued above the specified value as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

2. The Plaintiff is a company registered under the Companies Act and the Defendant is a limited liability partnership, both being persons sui juris competent to institute and defend this suit respectively.
3. The cause of action arose within the territorial limits of this Hon'ble Court as the contract was executed in Bengaluru, the Defendant's office is located at Whitefield, Bengaluru, and the performance was to be rendered within Bengaluru, invoking Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

LIMITATION

4. The suit is within limitation under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

5. The Plaintiff states that {{MEDIATION_COMPLIANCE_STATEMENT -- To be completed: Either (a) Pre-institution mediation was conducted before {{MEDIATION_AUTHORITY}} on {{DATE}} and concluded with {{OUTCOME}}, certificate enclosed as Annexure F; OR (b) This suit is filed with an application for urgent interim relief exempting pre-institution mediation under Section 12A(3) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.}}

FACTS OF THE CASE

6. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a written Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of an ERP system at a contract value of Rs. 45,00,000/-, as evidenced by Annexure A.
7. Pursuant to the agreement, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000/- as the first milestone payment via NEFT (UTR: ICIC20240201456789) on 01.02.2024, evidenced by Annexure B.
8. The Defendant was contractually bound to deliver the first module of the ERP system by 01.05.2024, and both parties were competent to contract under the applicable laws governing companies and limited liability partnerships.
9. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024, constituting a material breach of the essential terms of the agreement.
10. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant abandoned the project without delivering any module or refunding the advance payment, as evidenced by the email correspondence in Annexure C.
11. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 demanding performance and refund, recorded in Annexure E, to which the Defendant has failed to respond or remedy the breach.

LOSS AND DAMAGE

12. The Plaintiff claims refund of the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid to the Defendant, which remains unjustly retained following the abandonment of the project.
13. The Plaintiff suffered consequential damages of Rs. 12,00,000/- due to delay in operations caused by the non-delivery of the ERP system, quantified in the loss assessment report by auditor at Annexure D.
14. The Plaintiff took steps to mitigate loss by engaging an auditor to assess the financial impact of the breach, and the claimed damages are directly causally linked to the Defendant's failure to perform.

LEGAL BASIS

15. The Plaintiff is entitled to relief under Section 37 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, as the parties are bound to perform their respective promises unless dispensed with or excused.
16. Time was of the essence in the contract, and the Defendant's failure to deliver by 01.05.2024 invokes Section 55 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 regarding liability for non-performance within a fixed time.
17. Under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, the Plaintiff is entitled to compensation for any loss or damage caused by the breach of contract, including consequential damages reasonably arising from the breach.
18. The contract is valid and enforceable with no arbitration clause barring this suit, and there has been no novation, accord, or satisfaction discharging the original contractual obligations.

CAUSE OF ACTION

19. The cause of action first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project and failed to deliver the module, giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to sue for breach and recovery.
20. The suit is filed within the limitation period prescribed by Article 55 of the Limitation Act, 1963, being within three years from the date of the breach of contract.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

21. The total suit valuation is Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) for the purpose of jurisdiction and court fee, comprising the refund and consequential damages.
22. The requisite court fee of {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid under {{COURT_FEE_ACT_NAME}}, and the plaint is properly stamped and tendered with the requisite court fee.

PRAYER

23. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:
(a) Award damages in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only);
(b) Award interest pendente lite and future interest at 18% per annum on the said amount;
(c) Award future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit from the date of decree till realisation under Section 34 CPC;
(d) Award costs of the suit including advocate's fees to the Plaintiff;
(e) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Software Development Agreement dated 01.02.2024

Annexure B — NEFT receipt (UTR: ICIC20240201456789)

Annexure C — Email correspondence showing abandonment

Annexure D — Loss assessment report by auditor

Annexure E — Legal notice dated 01.07.2024

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 23 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

STATEMENT OF TRUTH

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff / authorised representative of the Plaintiff herein, do hereby state on solemn affirmation that the contents of the above plaint and the facts stated in paragraphs 1 to 23 are true to my knowledge and belief and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

I understand that I shall be liable to proceedings under law for making a false statement.

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

                                    {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

                                    PLAINTIFF

                                    THROUGH:
                                    {{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
                                    Advocate for the Plaintiff
                                    Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
